title: The US going 100% EV by 2040 would save more than 100k lives, study says
gist: An ICCT/FIA Foundation model finds ambitious US EV adoption could prevent 108,400 premature deaths and 42,100 pediatric asthma cases by 2050, with heavy-duty diesel vehicles disproportionately responsible for tailpipe pollution and zero-emission truck adoption showing early growth.

insights.claim: The ICCT estimates ambitious US EV adoption could prevent 108,400 premature deaths and 42,100 new pediatric asthma cases by 2050, versus over 41,800 US premature deaths currently attributable to road-transport air pollution.
insights.speaker: Jonathan M. Gitlin
insights.so_what: Quantifies a public-health argument for EV policy independent of climate, strengthening the cost-benefit case regulators use to justify mandates and subsidies.
insights.quote: Ambitious EV adoption still has the potential to help prevent 108,400 premature deaths and 42,100 new pediatric asthma cases in the US by 2050.
insights.locator: The current health impact from transportation pollution

insights.claim: Road-transport pollution caused nearly 700,000 premature deaths worldwide and nearly 250,000 new pediatric asthma cases in 2024, with China leading deaths and the US topping new asthma cases at 23,100.
insights.speaker: Jonathan M. Gitlin
insights.so_what: Global scale of transport-pollution mortality frames the total addressable public-health benefit driving EV and clean-freight policy worldwide.
insights.locator: The current health impact from transportation pollution amounted to almost 700,000

insights.claim: Even under the baseline 2025 scenario (a US government hostile to clean energy), the US and other wealthy nations should still see a roughly 50 percent reduction in premature deaths and slightly greater cuts in pediatric asthma cases.
insights.speaker: Jonathan M. Gitlin
insights.so_what: Suggests fleet turnover and tightening standards deliver health gains even absent aggressive policy, a baseline for de-risking EV-adoption forecasts.
insights.locator: Even under the baseline 2025 scenario, the US and other wealthy nations

insights.claim: Heavy-duty vehicles are only about 1 in 20 vehicles but account for 36% of transport energy consumption, 60% of tailpipe NOx, 55% of tailpipe PM2.5, and 65% of tailpipe SO2, concentrating pollution in the diesel truck fleet.
insights.speaker: Jonathan M. Gitlin
insights.so_what: Identifies heavy-duty freight electrification as the highest-leverage pollution-reduction target, directing capital toward electric truck and charging infrastructure.
insights.quote: heavy-duty vehicles are responsible for 36 percent of transport energy consumption, 60 percent of tailpipe NOx, 55 percent of tailpipe PM2.5, and 65 percent of tailpipe SO2
insights.locator: A disproportionate amount of pollution comes from heavy-duty

insights.claim: Two- and three-wheelers, though just 4% of transport energy consumption, contribute 14% of tailpipe PM2.5, 19% of tailpipe VOC, and 12% of tailpipe CO, making them a disproportionately dirty segment.
insights.speaker: Jonathan M. Gitlin
insights.so_what: Flags two/three-wheeler electrification (large in Asia) as an underappreciated pollution-reduction and market opportunity.
insights.locator: Two- and three-wheelers are also rather dirty

insights.claim: US zero-emission heavy-truck adoption reached 4% in the second half of 2025, with a cumulative fleet of 72,308 by December 2025, nearly 20,000 more than end-2024, showing growth despite absent tax credits or corporate ESG mandates.
insights.speaker: Jonathan M. Gitlin
insights.so_what: Concrete adoption momentum for electric trucks even without incentives suggests underlying economics, not subsidies, are increasingly driving demand.
insights.quote: Zero-emissions heavy truck adoption reached 4 percent in the second half of 2025, with a cumulative total by December last year of 72,308 nationwide.
insights.locator: While there is a growing number of zero-emissions heavy-duty vehicles

insights.claim: ICCT program director Ray Minjares argues zero-emission freight already makes economic sense across a growing number of routes, especially where diesel health impacts are greatest.
insights.speaker: Jonathan M. Gitlin
insights.so_what: Route-level cost parity is the tipping-point signal for fleet electrification; watch corridor economics rather than national averages.
insights.quote: Zero-emission freight makes economic sense across a growing number of routes, especially where diesel health impacts are greatest

insights.claim: In lower-income regions, transport pollution could rise 50 percent or more due to lax regulations and slower vehicle replacement, whereas ambitious global EV adoption would let even the poorest countries match the pollution cuts wealthy nations achieve in the baseline.
insights.speaker: Jonathan M. Gitlin
insights.so_what: Highlights emerging-market pollution divergence, framing where policy intervention and clean-vehicle demand growth will matter most.
insights.locator: Even under the baseline 2025 scenario, high-income regions
